note: Generated /use-cases/* pages. Slugs MUST NOT collide with hand-authored use-case pages (agentic-coding, ai-saas, capture-and-distill, embedded, enterprise-search, mobile, web3-verifiable, index).

usecases.title: Customer support deflection
usecases.problem: High-volume support teams pay per token to a frontier model for answers that follow a narrow, repetitive playbook. The bill scales with ticket volume and the model is generic, not tuned to your product or tone.
usecases.bluf: kolm captures your real support conversations, distills them into a small specialist that asks the right clarifying question first, and quantizes it to your own hardware — so support answers run at fixed cost with a signed build receipt instead of a per-ticket token bill.
usecases.kolm_flow: Point your support assistant at the kolm gateway. It captures (question, resolution) pairs with PII redacted on the way in. Once the lake is full, kolm distills a 7B specialist, gates it on the K-score, and compiles a quantized artifact you serve yourself.
usecases.proof_stat_context: The trinity-500 support specialist asks the right clarifying question 96.5% of the time and never invents an order it was not given, at 1.24 s mean latency.
usecases.outcome_bluf: The result is a support model you own: it follows your playbook, runs on hardware you control, and every build is signed and replayable — eliminating the per-token bill that scaled with ticket volume.

faqs.q: Will the specialist hallucinate fewer details?
faqs.a: In the published benchmark the distilled specialist invented no fabricated details (100% no-inventions) and asked for missing information rather than guessing.

faqs.q: How much support traffic do I need?
faqs.a: Distillation works from a few hundred high-quality captured pairs; the trinity-500 benchmark used a council-distilled set evaluated on 57 held-out cases.
Yes. The compiled specialist is a portable .kolm artifact that runs in your VPC or air-gapped, with no per-token bill.

usecases.title: Structured document extraction
usecases.problem: Extracting fields from contracts, claims, or filings with a frontier model is expensive at scale and exposes sensitive document text to a third-party API on every call.
usecases.bluf: kolm captures your extraction calls, redacts PII inline, distills a specialist tuned to your schema, and quantizes it for on-prem use — so document extraction runs privately at fixed cost with a verifiable build receipt.
usecases.kolm_flow: Route extraction prompts through the kolm gateway so each (document, extracted-fields) pair is captured and redacted. kolm distills a specialist, gates it on a calibrated K-score, and compiles a quantized model you run inside your own boundary.
usecases.proof_stat_context: On the public redaction benchmark, inline PII detection scored F1 1.0 with zero false positives across the fixture, so sensitive document text is scrubbed before storage.
usecases.outcome_bluf: You end up with an extraction model you own and run privately, with PII redacted on capture and a signed receipt for every compile — replacing a per-document API bill with fixed-cost local inference.

faqs.q: Does document text leave my environment?
faqs.a: The gateway and lake are self-hostable, and PII is redacted on the capture path. You can run the whole pipeline inside your own boundary.
kolm gates releases on a calibrated K-score and lets you hold out an eval set, so a specialist only ships if it clears your quality bar.
The compiled model exports as GGUF, EXL2, or a vLLM-servable artifact, and runs with no kolm dependency at inference time.

usecases.title: Internal code review assistant
usecases.problem: A frontier-model code reviewer is costly per pull request and sends proprietary source to an external API. The reviews are generic rather than tuned to your codebase conventions.
usecases.bluf: kolm captures your review interactions, distills a specialist that learns your conventions, and quantizes it for local serving — so PR review runs on your own hardware at fixed cost with a signed receipt, and your source never has to leave on every call.
usecases.kolm_flow: Point your review tool's LLM at the kolm gateway. Captured (diff, review) pairs feed a distillation run; kolm compiles a quantized specialist you serve in CI or on a workstation.
usecases.proof_stat_context: A capable 32B reasoning base compresses to 17.9 GB INT4 in the verified matrix, small enough to serve a code reviewer on a single workstation GPU.
usecases.outcome_bluf: You get a review assistant you own, tuned to your conventions, served locally at fixed cost, with a signed receipt proving exactly which weights were compiled.

faqs.a: Yes. The compiled artifact serves an OpenAI-compatible endpoint via kolm serve, which a CI job can call locally.

faqs.q: Does my source go to a third party?
faqs.a: With a self-hosted gateway and a compiled local model, inference happens inside your boundary. PII and secrets are redacted on capture.
You choose the base and quantization. The verified matrix records sizes from 0.44 GB (a 0.5B model INT4) up to 17.9 GB (a 32B model INT4).

usecases.title: On-prem internal chat assistant
usecases.problem: Regulated teams cannot send internal questions to a public LLM API, yet hosting a generic open model gives weak, untuned answers and still carries operational cost.
usecases.bluf: kolm distills an internal-chat specialist from your captured Q&A and quantizes it to run fully on-prem — so a regulated team gets a tuned assistant inside its own boundary, with PII redacted on capture and a signed receipt for every build.
usecases.kolm_flow: Run the kolm gateway inside your network. Capture internal (question, answer) pairs with redaction, distill a specialist, gate it on the K-score, and compile a quantized artifact that serves with no outbound network.
usecases.proof_stat_context: The distilled specialist answers at 1.24 s mean latency, fast enough for interactive internal chat without any external API.
usecases.outcome_bluf: The outcome is an internal assistant that runs air-gapped on your own hardware, tuned to your knowledge, with a verifiable receipt and no per-token bill.

faqs.q: Can this run with no internet?
faqs.a: Yes. Once compiled, the artifact serves locally with no network dependency at inference time.

faqs.q: Is it tuned to our content?
faqs.a: Distillation trains on your captured pairs, so the specialist reflects your internal knowledge and tone rather than a generic base model.

faqs.q: How do we audit a build?
faqs.a: Every compile emits an Ed25519-signed receipt with weight hashes and the K-score, replayable at /verify-prod.
